speaker: interviewer
text: Ellen Picker. You were telling me about your leaving Russia and it was in 1947.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it must have been January. We arrived in March in Vienna, and we were greeted by the mayor of-- of Vienna. First of all, they came into the train and pfft, pfft, [INAUDIBLE] everything, they came and [LAUGHS] we were disinfected, and then there was a speech on the-- on the platform.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came into-- into a home for homeless people, but this was a beautiful home. It was a home from very wealthy Jewish people, the [INAUDIBLE], very near where-- where Schubert was, and you know? And this was in two buildings and we lived two or three families in a room. The rooms were very big.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my father, you know, he was very clever man, a very, very clever man. Crafty. Got a beautiful home in Vienna, a luxury home, and we moved out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: The marriage? The marriage was very-- I had shoes with holes-- or wooden soles. Somebody lend me a dress, and we  had a silver ring, which I still have. It is very, very thin. And-- and it was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think that there was some special--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, yes, a rabbi. Before it's-- before I was married, he called me. He called me to his private home, and he said, are you Jewish? I said, yes, I am, because I feel like-- I felt like it. And he said-- and then he asked me, what do Jewish people eat at Pesach? Things like that, and other things, which I all knew. And-- and then he-- he went back and I heard, he said, see that? She said she's Jewish [LAUGHS] to his wife-- to his wife. [LAUGHS] And so-- I felt-- I felt Jewish. I am so Jewish that no other Jew can be more Jewish. Not religiously. Actually, I don't believe in religions whatsoever. They only did bad in this world, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think you-- you and your husband were the first Jews to be married in the synagogue--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Vienna after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everything was borrowed, but it was lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We love-- it was a great love. We still love each other. There is not a day that we don't declare our love to each other. When-- when-- when we meet at night in the corridor, we will go to the toilet, we embrace, you know, it's-- it's wonderful, it's absolutely-- where does it happen? We're nearly 50 years married. It's wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you learn of what had happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, then we-- when we-- when we pass the Russian border to-- to-- where did we go? To-- what is it called? Where the [INAUDIBLE] and his wife were-- were sentenced to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: Romania, and the Joint was always there. You can telegraph. We sent a telegraph every-- and we telegraph and we telegraph, remember, and no answer. And then we came to Vienna and no answer, and we  went and then came the news that he died. He died the same day when we got a letter. Then we got all his things back, and we saw by that that he was well-dressed and-- and the letters that people thanked him for he gave his cards away for food and things like that. He was such an angel, such a wonderful-- but he had a lot of friends. The letters he got from people, you know what it was. He had a lot of friends. And everybody always, I hope you have more luck next time, because this war  is-- he always was worried about us. They knew that he was searching and searching and searching.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in Vienna after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. My-- my father-in-law used to be a plumber, and the Jewish-- the Jewish-- [NON-ENGISH] What is it? I ask it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Welfare.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welfare helped him to establish himself, and he got very good jobs from the government, because there is lots of metal roofing in Vienna. Coppered. He had a few people working for him. He got up very early in the morning. He was very well-dressed. Everything is monitored. His women are not allowed to wear anything but gold, and he was a real snob. [LAUGHS] Anyhow, he worked himself up at a very well-known theater in Vienna, [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember that you heard of it, but the best comedians, and a cabaret sort of thing. And people who came to Vienna wanted definitely to go there. It was, like, you know, we have to see that. And-- but his-- his plumbing business was always very good. And he came visiting us here a few times, and he loved it here. He wanted us to come back. He wanted to give Bert a coffeehouse. He had a coffeehouse, just to come back, but we never wanted to come back, and we are so happy that we didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We tried him to get here, but it was-- Bert even went to Canberra, and she-- she couldn't come. She was born in Russia. So stupid. And they couldn't come here. They wouldn't give the-- now it would be different, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Vienna for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We straightaway wanted to go away, and we-- Bert had a cousin here, and still has, and they helped us to come for a new permit and everything. So we-- we had a permit. My mother didn't get it. We only applied for her later. And we came here, and we didn't like it. It was so hot. And I said, Bert, we just save [INAUDIBLE] I can't stand it. We stopped in-- in-- in this hot country somewhere, and we got a bean sandwich. [LAUGHS] It was all the time, [INAUDIBLE] We got a bean sandwich-- noodles with tomato or sandwich, and the flies came from everywhere. It was horrible. I just wanted to go back, but here it was better, you see. We got straightaway jobs in-- in-- in factories, and we worked all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Once we worked together in a factory. He was a presser. It was labor generally, and I did-- all in Jewish firms. And then I work at [INAUDIBLE], and then at AWA. You know, when you didn't like one job, you can go next door and get another one. And Bert had a job with his cousin. And then my mother and I started a business, and-- but we were afraid to take Bert in, because we didn't know whether it will carry us all, but when it became busier, then he said to his cousin he can't work full-time, and he only wanted him full-time, so he left, and we did our own business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of business was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Curtains, bedspreads, things like that. It was very good. We had-- we had wonderful customers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three. Three girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One, my eldest girl is in Sydney, married to a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? John ?] Vinen. And he is a director in North Shore Hospital in the trauma department. And now my second daughter is in America married to [? John ?] Norton. Very good family. My-- my children are all happy, that's the main thing. And this daughter has two children, and she has two children in America. He's a professor there in-- in a university, and she's an architect. She works also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my third daughter is in Melbourne. She plays with the symphony orchestra. She has a de facto husband, is very happy, and has a lovely child. You see, he's an artist. She's an artist. They don't have to marry, you know? [LAUGHS] Do you think that what you went through affected the way you brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were-- they were always very stricted. I would-- they had to-- for instance, they got very little pocket money. I gave them, later on, a yearly allowance of $70. They had to dress themselves for the-- but the shoes, the shoes we bought. And they can also beautiful, and they can do what they like. Now they don't do it, but they did. They dressed themselves lovely with nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and I don't want to go. My cousins tell me it's still very many bad people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel now, 50 years after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe it-- I-- I don't believe it. It was another life. It's unbelievable when you think back, you know, that you went through that. I mean, there were so many things happening, little-- little things you laugh about now, but they were very terrible when it happened, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the effect of all of this on your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had always pretty sheltered life even in-- you know, in camp it was terrible, in the cells it was terrible. You lay there day in, day out, and bored, and when you were allowed to wash the floors, the prisoners' floors, outside in the corridor, that was like an overseas trip. And you have to-- you had to wash it with your own garments, and that was something out of that you could go out of the cell. That was something. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you kept in the cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you washed-- when you washed something in the cell, you got-- you got twice hot water, or three times hot water a day. So you took the hot water in the mouth. You put it on your garment, and put it on the floor where we washed it like that. And then my mother and I would stand like that and dry it. We didn't have any garments, because the shoes, ah, we went once to a-- an [INAUDIBLE] in a train, and we were together with a girl who was with us in the same prison, and this girl, first of all, she had-- she had the child, and she pressed her breast and the milk came out, and she always went out at night to give the-- the boarders, most probably. She got something, they got something. And then suddenly, we-- we went to another prison with her. And she had told the whole-- everybody that she had murdered a old couple, and she told us exactly what she did. And she said, if you murder something, the photograph of the murderer will be in the eyes of the-- of the murdered person. So she took the eyes out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine? With this person, we sat in the-- in the wagon, and it was a-- it was a prison train with the-- with a thing where you walk and then the separate compartments. But there were this prison gates on the-- on the windows and also on front. And we sat there alone with the-- in this-- in this compartment, and my mother and I, we sat close together. We were so frightened. And in this wagon, I found under-- under the seat, I found a-- a sort of a piece of material, and it was a sack of a very good material, white, like a very heavy white linen. But it was all full of shit. [LAUGHS] But I took it, and I had a skirt later on with it. And you know how-- how we did the needles? From combs.

speaker: interviewee
text: You take two-- two coins and one tooth of a comb, and you filed it and filed it till it was very, very sharp. I don't know how we did the hole, but the hole has to be done too, and then you took one thread out of the material and you sewed with that. And you could make fire. We took a little bit of cotton wool out of our garment, we put a little bit of tools in-- into it, and took the lid off of the toilet, and rolled around the floor, and when you put it apart, [BLOWS] it was-- it-- it was glowing. And we-- we smoked cigarettes of material. You know, pieces of anything, we smoked it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I met people. And-- and-- and I met, in one camp, I met very famous wives of the most famous Communists at that time. One was Mrs. Bukharin. He was-- he was killed by Stalin because he was-- he would have been the leader of Russia otherwise. And one wife of-- wife of Bela Kun, that was the most famous Communist in-- in Hungary, and she was still a Communist, no matter what there. And-- and a few German women from Communist people. They were all separated from their husbands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, I saw a program on-- on SBS about Russia. This was a year ago even. And there was a woman interviewed-- I didn't recognize her, of course. She's an old woman now-- and it was-- it was the same woman from the-- the wife of this famous Russian, and she told her story, and then I heard her name, and I knew it was her. Strange. And I read about him and his story in the paper. I-- I cut it all out and keep it, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Life was very, very, difficult, and things we had to work with, absolutely unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unbelievable that they could do that with us. And yet, other people I-- I've spoken to were free, and they said it wasn't too bad. They didn't work that hard. They lived four families in a house with four rooms. I don't know where they were or why-- what-- In our camp were lots of Communists. There were lots of Polish people from-- who-- who were in a-- in an army, a Polish army against Hitler, what have you. And then there were a lot of Spaniards who were-- who were Communists, fled Spain, and they were all pilots. I don't know what they did, and then suddenly they were imprisoned. I don't know why. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So many Communists were imprisoned. But Stalin-- I don't know. He needed workers, and he imprisoned some. He took prisoners. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My children always wanted me to-- to write a book and I never did. Now they will have the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to your children much about what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but you know, if you don't-- if you are set-- if you are not hungry, you don't know how the hungry people feel. It's different.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very good thing happened to us women, we had-- we had no periods. I don't know, was it due to something they gave us in the food or because we were undernourished? It was wonderful. I don't know what we would've done. The whole time, nothing. Do you think about those times very often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very often and I have dreams sometimes, dreams they are-- they are coming to get me. It's like that, you know? A dream. You can't forget that this is-- this-- this six years, this concentrated horror. You can't-- you know, the sleeping arrangements, that is something out of this world. That you didn't get anything to sleep? Just the floor? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Year in and year out?

speaker: interviewer
text: Has it had an effect on your health, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? I-- I don't blame it, no. Look, my mother is nearly 100 years old. I remember, yeah, we-- we once walked also maybe 60 kilometers, and in front of-- and she was very weak, and they wanted her to take in a-- in a carriage, and we wouldn't part. So she made it to the prison gate and collapsed there. We didn't part. Once they wanted to part us, they put-- put in front with my mother, and  I said, you can shoot us but we won't part. And they didn't part us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that because you were together was the reason for your survival?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it is-- it was survival, the two us, you  know? And another thing, once when we went to another prison, also, a long walk, maybe 100 people, and suddenly we stopped. And the soldier read out a name, and this woman went out and she said, you are free, here in this-- this vicinity. And she went out, but she could speak Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a few kilometers later, he calls out my mother and me, and he said, and what's with you? And I-- I know that Russian Jew are you're interned, that we are interned. I said, we are interned. I didn't want to stay I said, what would we have done? We would have-- I don't-- you stood in-- in a step and you were free to go. Where to? In the camp, we had the worst crying at night, you know? And oh, this was the other thing. Lots of wolves. Are there any other stories that you remember that you want to tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember we worked once in a-- in a place where you-- where you have-- where you-- it was a seedlings on the grass under glass, and I needed to go to the toilet, and I looked out. This was-- around was nature. And I looked around and I saw a few trees further on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went and I saw a deep hole, very dry cracked top. You know, like you see sometimes on pictures, cracked top. And I went in and I sank into-- into-- I sank that far into-- from the toilet's sewage. Can you imagine that? And the only clothing I had was I had them on. It was terrible. I laugh now, but it was terrible. And then it was a camp. It was not far away. And I took my clothes off. There was a little brook going through the camp. My mother put the clothes in the brook, and the water, and so on, and so on. It was terrible, a terrible thing. Is that-- am I allowed to play with that? No I'm not. You would have lots of noises. Mrs. Picker, I'd like to thank you very much for telling us your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Will it fit on-- on one tape?

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Picker, can you introduce me to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my mother, Elizabeth Proskauer, and this is my husband, Bert Picker, Berthold Picker, but say Bert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is your mother now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is 90-- in four months, she will be 300-- 300, [LAUGHS] 100 in four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she went through everything with you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: --together.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to show you some things she did when we came. No, the good things are all lost, the things she did, the beautiful things she did are all lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she talk about those times with you at all or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not anymore. We have lots of friends. The people who were together in Russia is a family, and we always correspond with [INAUDIBLE], with Germany, with everywhere where they are, we are a big family. We know from everybody what's happened, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's lived with you always?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: She has a very good son-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only one year we were not together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came here one year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our first married year, we weren't.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photograph? Who's in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter in America with her family, her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name is Evelyn, Eve. She calls herself Eve. Her husband is [? John ?] Norton, and the children are Jonathan and Josephine Norton.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Pittsburgh where she-- where he is a professor on the university. My daughter is an architect, but she is now doing-- what is she doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Development-- develop-- development.

speaker: interviewee
text: Development, she's doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my eldest daughter-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about this photograph now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this photograph shows my daughter Susie with her husband and two children, [? John ?] Vinen, and Nicholas Vinen, and Katie Vinen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couple of years ago. But he  is now 17, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my youngest grandson. His name is Thomas Picker [INAUDIBLE]. That's how he says it, John. He always says, Thomas [? John ?] Picker [INAUDIBLE] is his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my youngest daughter, Michelle Picker with her son Thomas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photograph now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father Arthur Bruno Proskauer, it must-- the photo must have been taken in-- in London between '41 and '45, which from the beginning was probably '42 maybe. He still looks very well in this photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
